course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Determinants and Invertibility
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to compute determinants, use determinant properties, and decide when a matrix is invertible.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with: determinant zero means the matrix collapses space and is not invertible.
Write: det(A) nonzero means invertible for square matrices.
sayThisFirst: The determinant is a number attached to a square matrix.
For a 2D matrix, determinant tells area scaling and orientation.
For a square matrix, determinant nonzero means invertible.
Determinant zero means the columns are dependent.
Row swaps, row scaling, and row replacement affect determinants in different ways.
boardPlan: Write the invertible matrix test: det(A) != 0 means invertible.
Problem 1: compute a 3 by 3 determinant using expansion or row reduction.
Problem 2: track determinant changes from row operations.
Problem 3: find the k value that makes determinant zero.

keyFormulas.name: Invertibility test
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Nonzero determinant means the matrix has an inverse.

keyFormulas.name: Row swap
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: A row swap multiplies determinant by -1.

keyFormulas.name: Row scaling
keyFormulas.latex: R_i\leftarrow cR_i \Rightarrow \det \text{ is multiplied by } c
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Scaling a row scales the determinant.

keyFormulas.name: Row replacement
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Adding a multiple of one row to another does not change determinant.

commonMistakes: Using determinant tests on non-square matrices.
Forgetting that row replacement does not change the determinant.
Forgetting that a row swap changes the sign.
Saying determinant zero means no solutions for every Ax=b. More precisely, it means A is not invertible.
Dropping a negative sign during cofactor expansion.
notationWarnings: Use \det(A), not |A| if students may confuse it with absolute value.
For parameter problems, write \det(A(k))=0 to find non-invertible values.
Always mention square matrix before saying determinant/invertible.

problems.title: Compute a determinant and decide invertibility
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: determinant is just a number. For a square matrix, nonzero means invertible.

drawInstructions: Write the 3 by 3 matrix large on the board.
Choose the first row for cofactor expansion because it has a zero.
Circle the zero entry to show why that term disappears.
After finding det(A)=-15, write nonzero -> invertible.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the minus sign on the middle cofactor.
Saying invertible only because the matrix is 3 by 3. It is invertible because det is nonzero.

problems.title: Track determinant changes from row operations
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Suppose \(A\) is a \(3\times 3\) matrix with \(\det(A)=4\). A matrix \(B\) is made from \(A\) by doing these row operations in order:
1. Swap rows 1 and 2.
2. Multiply row 3 by 5.
3. Replace row 1 by row 1 plus 2 times row 2.
Find \(\det(B)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: do not try to invent A. Just track what each row operation does to the determinant.

drawInstructions: Label row replacement as no change.

walkthrough.label: Apply the row swap
walkthrough.latex: Swapping two rows multiplies the determinant by \(-1\):\n\[4\mapsto -4.\]

walkthrough.label: Apply row scaling
walkthrough.latex: Multiplying one row by 5 multiplies the determinant by 5:\n\[-4\mapsto -20.\]

walkthrough.label: Apply row replacement
walkthrough.latex: Replacing a row by itself plus a multiple of another row does not change the determinant. So it stays \(-20\).

commonMistakes: Thinking row replacement changes the determinant.
Forgetting row swap changes the sign.
Multiplying by 5 three times instead of once. Only one row was scaled.

problems.title: Find when a matrix is invertible
problems.studentPromptLatex:
For what values of \(k\) is
\[A(k)=\begin{bmatrix}1&2&k\\0&1&3\\2&0&1\end{bmatrix}\]
invertible?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: invertible means determinant nonzero. So find det(A(k)), set it not equal to zero.

drawInstructions: After computing 13-2k, draw a number line and mark k=13/2 as the bad value.
Say: every other value is okay.

walkthrough.label: Use determinant test
walkthrough.latex: A square matrix is invertible exactly when its determinant is nonzero.

walkthrough.label: Compute the determinant
walkthrough.latex: Expand along the second row or compute directly:\n\[\det(A(k))=13-2k.\]

walkthrough.label: Find the bad value
walkthrough.latex: The matrix is not invertible when\n\[13-2k=0.\]\nSo \(2k=13\), and \(k=\frac{13}{2}\).

walkthrough.label: State the invertible values
walkthrough.latex: Therefore, \(A(k)\) is invertible for every \(k\) except \(\frac{13}{2}\).

commonMistakes: Giving the non-invertible value when the question asks for invertible values.
Forgetting to say k not equal to 13/2.
Setting determinant equal to 1 instead of nonzero.

practiceLabPointer.note: More parameter determinant problems are in Section 4.

wrapUpQuestions: What determinant value means not invertible?
Which row operation does not change determinant?
What does a row swap do to determinant?
For parameter problems, what equation finds the bad values?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 4
linksToWebsite.reason: More determinant, invertibility, and transformation practice.

qualityControlNotes: Problem 3 checked: det(A(k))=13-2k, so non-invertible when k=13/2.
reviewSummary: Session 08 keeps determinant rules simple and checks all arithmetic before export.
